topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The vase sat on the reclaimed wood table, a vibrant, almost offensively cheerful arrangement of sunflowers and deep purple irises. I picked up the stems, my fingers tracing the smooth, cool petals. There was no note. Just the flowers, a silent, extravagant declaration. My gaze flicked around the apartment—at the piles of unfiled papers, the perpetually dusty bookshelf, the faint stain on my favorite sweater. Why would anyone send something so… bright? It felt like an imposition, a spotlight shining directly onto the mess I usually kept hidden. I tucked the bouquet awkwardly under my arm, the stems digging slightly into my palm. I walked toward the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, wondering if this gesture was some kind of elaborate, misplaced attempt to fix something that was fundamentally broken within me.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed me the wrapped arrangement, the sudden weight in my hands made me flinch. The colors were too bold, too perfect, and the absence of any accompanying message felt like an accusation. I stood frozen in the hallway, the scent of lilies suddenly overpowering. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick thing in my chest. I glanced down at the wrapping paper, scrutinizing the neat bow. *Too much,* a voice whispered in my head. *This is too much effort for someone who doesn't deserve it.* I managed a tight, almost brittle smile when I turned to thank the courier, my voice sounding unnaturally high. I quickly retreated to the living room, placing the flowers on the coffee table as if they were fragile evidence, unwilling to let anyone see the vulnerability they represented.

[story 3]

I found the flowers tucked into the entryway, resting beside my worn welcome mat. They were undeniably beautiful—a cascade of roses in shades I couldn't quite name. My stomach tightened, a familiar knot forming just beneath my ribs. I picked them up slowly, my movements stiff, as if handling something delicate and potentially damaging. I kept my eyes downcast, focusing intently on the pattern of the floorboards. Every nerve ending seemed to be screaming that this kindness was misplaced, an undeserved luxury in a life defined by quiet shortcomings. I didn't want to acknowledge the sender; acknowledging it meant admitting that perhaps, just perhaps, I wasn't entirely unworthy of such attention. I carried the bouquet toward the kitchen, the weight of the unspoken expectation pressing down on my shoulders.
